title: Conceptual and Geometric Foundations for a Teleparallel Approach to Quantum Gravity
abstract: We revisit quantum field theory in curved spacetime (QFTCS) as a semi-classical framework for quantum matter on classical geometries, emphasizing its limitations, including vacuum ambiguity and background dependence. We briefly review major approaches to quantum gravity (QG), including Loop Quantum Gravity (LQG), string theory, and asymptotic safety, highlighting their conceptual challenges. Motivated by these issues, we outline a teleparallel framework based on coframe and spin-connection variables, where gravity is encoded in torsion rather than curvature. This framework naturally incorporates local Lorentz symmetry and fermionic couplings while displaying a gauge-like structure. We argue that the coframe/spin-connection pair provides an alternative and geometrically refined description of gravitational variables, which may serve as a useful starting point for future investigations of QG. The purpose of this work is not to provide a complete quantization of teleparallel gravity but to identify the geometric and conceptual ingredients that such a formulation would require.
summary: This paper explores alternative geometric foundations for quantum gravity by focusing on teleparallel gravity, which replaces curvature with torsion as the fundamental field strength. It argues that using coframe and spin-connection variables provides a natural gauge structure suitable for quantization. The work aims to identify necessary conceptual ingredients rather than providing a complete solution to quantizing spacetime itself.
main_problem: The fundamental incompatibility between Quantum Field Theory (QFT) and General Relativity (GR) at the Planck scale, necessitating a new framework for quantizing gravity.
main_result: The paper proposes that reformulating gravity using coframe and spin-connection variables, where dynamics are governed by torsion rather than curvature, provides a geometrically refined starting point for quantum gravity research.
method: It compares various QG approaches (LQG, String Theory, Asymptotic Safety) and develops the teleparallel framework by treating the coframe/spin-connection pair as independent gauge variables.
model_or_system: The model is a theoretical extension of General Relativity into the teleparallel gravity sector, utilizing the coframe ($\mathbf{h}^a_\mu$) and spin-connection ($\omega^a_{b\mu}$) to encode gravitational dynamics in torsion. It analyzes matter coupling (Klein-Gordon, Dirac) within this framework.
key_observables: Torsion ($T$), Curvature ($R$), Nonmetricity ($Q$), and the constraint algebra generators ($\hat{H}, \hat{D}_i, \hat{J}^{ab}$).
important_parameters: Planck scale; coupling constants $\kappa$; mass $m$ (for Proca field).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that gravity can be reformulated using the coframe/spin-connection pair to achieve a gauge-like structure, bypassing some limitations of metric formulations.
figures_summary: Not specified in the notes; the text references comparative tables (TABLE I) summarizing different QG approaches based on their fundamental variables.
paper_structure: The paper first critiques QFTCS and reviews major QG candidates. It then introduces the teleparallel framework, detailing its geometric structure using coframe/spin-connection variables, deriving actions like $F(T)$, and finally outlining the canonical quantization program using constraint operators.
why_interesting: While focused on gravity, the development of a constrained quantum field theory based on gauge potentials (coframe/spin-connection) shares conceptual parallels with advanced topics in quantum information theory involving gauge symmetries and constraints.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
